title: Revitalizing the US Techno-Industrial Base
content:
During the Industrial Revolution, people with backgrounds in the trades frequently became the innovative engineers who pushed new inventions forward. A country whose citizens stop building and tinkering with technologies will inevitably lose its capacity to advance those technologies. Furthermore, a nation that allows its general industrial base to atrophy surrenders vital institutional knowledge and control over critical supply chains. This loss of overall depth and diversity in production makes it significantly harder to build strength in specific crucial sectors. The United States simply cannot allow this gradual erosion of its industrial capacity to happen.

To revitalize its techno-industrial base, Washington must pursue a national strategy with a comprehensive two-track approach. First, it must prioritize manufacturing by promoting innovation and advanced engineering across multiple sectors. Second, the government must fiercely protect those technological advances and know-how from unfair foreign competition and malign use. Promoting innovation demands that the United States much better leverage its human, financial, and strategic capital. This requires smart immigration policies that make it easier for the world's best scientific and engineering talent to come to the United States and stay.

A high concentration of leading engineers in key areas such as artificial intelligence currently hail from other countries. It is clearly in the fundamental U.S. interest for these highly skilled workers to build their careers and companies within the United States. Furthermore, the U.S. government should supercharge federal funding for research and development to restore the historic high levels seen in the 1960s. A major injection of basic research funding would provide a greater strategic return on investment than almost any other federal expenditure. Additionally, aggressive investment in clean energy projects is needed to provide the plentiful electric power required for advanced technologies like AI.

Recent legislative milestones demonstrate how targeted government action can effectively promote domestic manufacturing and innovation. The 2022 CHIPS and Science Act and the 2022 Inflation Reduction Act successfully focused on building capacity in the U.S. semiconductor and clean energy sectors. While these initial steps are highly encouraging, a much more comprehensive and integrated industrial strategy is still urgently needed. One powerful model for this is Operation Warp Speed, the initiative launched during the COVID-19 pandemic to rapidly develop and deploy a vaccine. Such focused, large-scale federal efforts show what is possible when the government actively coordinates industrial capabilities to meet urgent national challenges.
